← all tutorials

Lamport Logical Clocks — interactive LAMPORT_TIMESTAMPS.md lamport_timestamps.py

3 processes, 9 events, 2 messages. There is no global clock — we order events by causality. Toggle between physical time (unreliable wall-clock) and Lamport time (the logical clock L = max(local, msg) + 1) and watch the dots reposition. Click any event to light up its causal past, causal future, and concurrent events. All numbers recompute live from the identical scenario as lamport_timestamps.py.

① The timeline — toggle physical time ↔ Lamport time

What to do: flip between physical time (t) and Lamport time (L). In Lamport mode, c (t=6) slides left of h (t=8) because logical clocks track causal progress, not wall time. The two message arrows (b→e, f→h) are the only cross-process causal links.
click an event to explore happens-before ↓
local send receive causal past (→selected) causal future (selected→) concurrent (‖)

② Happens-before explorer — click an event above, see its causal cone

What this shows: for the selected event, its past (events that → it), future (events it →), and concurrent set (‖ — no causal link either way). The ⚠ marks the §4 trap: concurrent events whose Lamport clocks differ, so they look ordered but aren't. Try c: it has L=3 < L(f)=4 yet c ‖ f.
Click an event in the timeline above ↑

③ Total order — the (L, pid) tiebreaker linearizes the partial order

What this shows: sort by (L, pid). Events sharing an L (highlighted) are concurrent — the process id breaks the tie without ever violating causality. This single global order is what powers mutual exclusion (④).

④ Lamport mutual exclusion — min (timestamp, pid) wins the resource

What to do: each process broadcasts a REQUEST(timestamp, pid). Everyone orders the shared queue by the same (t, pid) rule, so they all agree who goes first — no leader, no votes. Drag the timestamps; the default (P1=1, P3=1, P2=2) matches lamport_timestamps.py Section E.
check: … JS recompute matches lamport_timestamps.py: clocks, 23 happens-before pairs, total order, and the gold clock condition (a→b ⟹ L(a)<L(b)).
